title: Equivalent expressions with common denominators (denominators 10 & 100)
description: Rewrite a fraction addition problem so the fractions have common denominators.  All items have denominators of either 10 or 100.
topic_path: Topic D: Addition with tenths and hundredths

hints:
To add fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac7{10}}+ \dfrac{13}{100}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}+ \dfrac{13}{100}$

This is the equivalent expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}+ \dfrac{13}{100}$

To add fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac4{10}}+ \dfrac{47}{100}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}+ \dfrac{47}{100}$

This is the equivalent expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}+ \dfrac{47}{100}$

To add fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

This is the equivalent expression.

assessment_items.question_content:
A bunny's ears grew $\dfrac{14}{100}\text{ cm}$ last year and $\dfrac4{10}\text{ cm}$ this year.

**What expression can be used to find the total length the bunny's ears grew during the two years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total amount the bunny's ear grew, we need to add $\dfrac{14}{100}\text{ cm}$ and $\dfrac4{10}\text{ cm}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$ in our expression.

$ \dfrac{14}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac4{10}}=  \dfrac{14}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$

The following expression can be used to find the total length the bunny's ears grew.

$\dfrac{14}{100}+\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A magic bean plant grew $\dfrac{97}{100}\text{ m}$ one minute and $\dfrac9{10}\text{ m}$ the next minute.

**What expression can be used to find the total amount the plant grew during the two minutes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total amount the plant grew, we need to add $\dfrac{97}{100}\text{ m}$ and $\dfrac9{10}\text{ m}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$ in our expression.

$ \dfrac{97}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac9{10}}=  \dfrac{97}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$

The following expression can be used to find the total amount the plant grew.

$\dfrac{97}{100}+\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In a snail race, one snail crawled $\dfrac{106}{100}\text{ m}$ in one minute. Another snail crawled $\dfrac8{10}\text{ m}$ in one  minute.

**What expression can be used to find the total distance both snails crawled in the race?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total distance both snails crawled in the race, we need to add $\dfrac{106}{100}\text{ m}$ and $\dfrac8{10}\text{ m}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{8}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{8}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{80}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{8}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{80}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{8}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{80}{100}}$ in our expression.

$ \dfrac{106}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac8{10}}=  \dfrac{106}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{80}{100}}$

The following expression can be used to find the total distance both snails crawled in the race.

$\dfrac{106}{100}+\blueD{\dfrac{80}{100}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Gus ate a stack of two pancakes for breakfast. One pancake was $\dfrac{130}{100}\text{ cm}$ thick and the other pancake was $\dfrac{15}{10}\text{ cm}$ thick.

**What expression can be used to find the total height of the stack?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total height of the two pancakes, we need to add $\dfrac{130}{100}\text{ cm}$ and $\dfrac{15}{10}\text{ cm}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{15}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]


[[☃ image 3]]


[[☃ image 4]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{15}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{150}{100}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{15}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{150}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{15}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{150}{100}}$ in our expression.

$ \dfrac{130}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{15}{10}}=  \dfrac{130}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{150}{100}}$

The following expression can be used to find the total height of the stack.

$\dfrac{130}{100}+ \blueD{\dfrac{150}{100}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Soshi made two big snowballs for a snowman. One snowball was $\dfrac{4}{10}\text{ m}$ tall and the other snowball was $\dfrac{65}{100}\text{ m}$ tall.

**What expression can be used to find the total height of both snowballs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total height of the two snowballs, we need to add $\dfrac{4}{10}\text{ m}$ and $\dfrac{65}{100}\text{ m}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac4{10}} + \dfrac{65}{100} = \blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}} +  \dfrac{65}{100} $

The following expression can be used to find the total height of both snowballs.

$\blueD{\dfrac{40}{100}}+\dfrac{65}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
On their way to a pond, one frog jumped $\dfrac{3}{10}\text{ m}$ and another frog jumped $\dfrac{25}{100}\text{ m}$.

**What expression can be used to find the total distance the frogs jumped?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total distance the frogs jumped, we need to add $\dfrac{3}{10}\text{ m}$ and $\dfrac{25}{100}\text{ m}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{30}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{30}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{30}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac3{10}} + \dfrac{25}{100} = \blueD{\dfrac{30}{100}} +  \dfrac{25}{100} $

The following expression can be used to find the total distance the frogs jumped.

$\blueD{\dfrac{30}{100}}+\dfrac{25}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Lim is growing two giant pumpkins in her backyard. One pumpkin is $\dfrac{5}{10}\text{ m}$ tall. The other pumpkin is $\dfrac{41}{100}\text{ m}$ tall.

**What expression can be used to find the total height of both pumpkins?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total height of both pumpkins, we need to add $\dfrac{5}{10}\text{ m}$ and $\dfrac{41}{100}\text{ m}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 
 

[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{50}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{50}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{50}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac5{10}} + \dfrac{41}{100} = \blueD{\dfrac{50}{100}} +  \dfrac{41}{100} $

The following expression can be used to find the total height of both pumpkins.

$\blueD{\dfrac{50}{100}}+\dfrac{41}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Joseph is eating his favorite kind of cookie. The cookie part is $\dfrac{9}{10}\text{ cm}$ thick. The frosting part is  $\dfrac{33}{100}\text{ cm}$ thick.

**What expression can be used to find the total thickness of the cookie and frosting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total height of both the cookie and frosting, we need to add $\dfrac{9}{10}\text{ cm}$ and $\dfrac{33}{100}\text{ cm}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac9{10}} + \dfrac{33}{100} = \blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}} +  \dfrac{33}{100} $

The following expression can be used to find the total thickness of the cookie and frosting.

$\blueD{\dfrac{90}{100}}+\dfrac{33}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Malati divides a deck of cards into two piles. One pile is $\dfrac{12}{10}\text{ cm}$ tall and the other pile is $\dfrac{89}{100}\text{ cm}$ tall.

**What expression can be used to find the total height of both piles of cards?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total height of both piles of cards, we need to add $\dfrac{12}{10}\text{ cm}$ and $\dfrac{89}{100}\text{ cm}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{12}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]


[[☃ image 3]]


[[☃ image 4]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{12}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{120}{100}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{12}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{120}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{12}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{120}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac{12}{10}} + \dfrac{89}{100} = \blueD{\dfrac{120}{100}} +  \dfrac{89}{100} $

The following expression can be used to find the total height of both piles of cards.

$\blueD{\dfrac{120}{100}}+\dfrac{89}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
One ladybug is $\dfrac{53}{100}\text{ cm}$ long. Another ladybug is $\dfrac{6}{10}\text{ cm}$ long.

**What expression can be used to find the total length of both ladybugs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find the total length of both ladybugs, we need to add $\dfrac{53}{100}\text{ cm}$ and $\dfrac{6}{10}\text{ cm}$.

To add the fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]
 

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{6}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{60}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{6}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{60}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{60}{100}}$ in our expression.

$ \dfrac{53}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac6{10}}=  \dfrac{53}{100} + \blueD{\dfrac{60}{100}}$

The following expression can be used to find the total length of both ladybugs.

$\dfrac{53}{100}+\blueD{\dfrac{60}{100}}$
